turns.content: I just refactored a match statement on an Option<T> where the None branch is literally impossible right now, so I used unreachable!() there. It compiles but feels wrong compared to the todo!() and unimplemented!() macros I've seen elsewhere. Is this actually safe or am I missing something about how these panic variants interact with type safety?

You are correct that unreachable!() is semantically different from todo!() and unimplemented!(), which is why it feels wrong to use it for a branch that could theoretically exist later. The key distinction is that unreachable!() signals code that will never execute, whereas todo!() and unimplemented!() signal incomplete implementations that are expected to be implemented eventually. If you mark an impossible path with todo!() or unimplemented!(), the compiler won't warn you about dead code because it assumes those branches might change in the future, which breaks your mental model of safety. This is why unreachable!() is only appropriate when you can prove logically that a condition will never be true and no one intends to change that logic later.
